fr:mainmatter.html:p: Now, let's look at our second example logic. This will work with a similar idea: we're going to think of valuations as something like possible worlds, and we're going to think of an argument as trying to guarantee the truth of its conclusion given the truth of all its premises. But we're now going to use strong Kleene valuations instead of Boolean valuations. Recall that the strong Kleene structure has three values: \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. We can assume that \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} still represents truth, and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} still falsity. But what about \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}? As I presented the strong Kleene structure last time, I mentioned that this value can be interpreted in different ways. For now, let's assume we're interpreting it as something that isn't true: perhaps as neither true nor false, say. So we're supposing that \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} is the only true value in this structure. Then in our new logic, a counterexample to \Gamma \;/\; A is a strong Kleene valuation that takes every premise in \Gamma to the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and takes A to either \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. This logic is called K3. (The "K" here is for "Kleene", and the "3" is for the number of values in the strong Kleene structure.)

As in classical logic, p \land q \;/\; p is valid in K3. By revisiting (or recalling) the strong Kleene table for \land , you can see that for any valuation v, if v(p) is either \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, then v(p \land q) must also be either \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. So there is no valuation that is a counterexample to this argument. Also as in classical logic, p \lor q \;/\; p is invalid in K3. For example, take a valuation w with w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and w(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. Then we have w(p \lor q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}; so this is a counterexample to the argument. (There is another value p could have in counterexamples to this argument as well; do you see it?)

Again as in classical logic, p, 
eg p \;/\; q is valid in K3. To have a K3 counterexample to this argument, we would need a strong Kleene valuation that assigned \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} to both p and 
eg p (as well as assigning some other value to q). But no strong Kleene valuation can assign \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} to both p and 
eg p. So there is no K3 counterexample to this argument.

Despite these similarities, however, classical logic and K3 are not the same. For example, we've seen that \;/\; p \lor 
eg p is classically valid. But it is not K3 valid. That is to say, p \lor 
eg p is not a theorem of K3. Take any strong Kleene valuation v where v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. Then since 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, we also have v(
eg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. And since \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} \lor \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, this gives v(p \lor 
eg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. That is, assuming our interpretation of \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} as neither true nor false, on any valuation where p itself is neither true nor false, p \lor 
eg p is also neither true nor false. And if it's neither true nor false, then in particular it ain't true. So we have a K3 counterexample to \;/\; p \lor 
eg p.

One way into the idea starts from thinking of our valuations again as possible worlds. For simplicitly, let's start here with Boolean valuations. Let's say we want to count an argument as valid when, no matter what, the truth of its premises guarantees the truth of its conclusion. We can use our valuations, standing in for possible worlds, to understand the "no matter what" part here.

As a warmup, say that a Boolean valuation v is a counterexample to an argument \Gamma \;/\; A when every premise in \Gamma gets the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} on valuation v and the conclusion A gets value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} on v. If an argument has any counterexamples, then we can see that the truth of its premises does not guarantee the truth of its conclusion---the counterexample shows a way to distribute truth values among the atomic formulas that makes the premises all true without making the conclusion true.

So we can get a logic from our Boolean valuations like this: if any Boolean valuation is a counterexample to an argument, this logic counts the argument as invalid. On the other hand, if no Boolean valuation is a counterexample to the argument, then this logic counts the argument as valid. This is a very widely used logic, called classical logic.

Recall the history here, though: this only goes back to the 19th century. So the "classical" here is more like the "classical" in "classical music" than it is like the "classical" in "classical architecture".

Let's return to some earlier example arguments, to see how they fare in classical logic. First, think about p \land q \;/\; p. To see whether this argument is classically valid or not, we should ask: is any Boolean valuation a counterexample to this argument? Remember, a counterexample to this argument is a valuation v where v(p \land q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. By looking at the Boolean structure, though, you can see that this is impossible: if v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then no matter what v(q) is we also have v(p \land q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. So no Boolean valuation is a counterexample to p \land q \;/\; p. As a result, this argument is classically valid.

For a contrasting example, think about p \lor q \;/\; p. Is any Boolean valuation a counterexample to this argument? A counterexample to this argument would be a valuation w where w(p \lor q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. Is there any such w? Yes, there is. Since there is always a valuation for any combination of values for our atomic sentences, consider any valuation w where w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} and w(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. In any such valuation, we have w(p \lor q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, so any such valuation is a counterexample to this argument. As a result, this argument is not classically valid.

Just like the Boolean structure, classical logic doesn't have to be interpreted as about truth. Recall that if we're thinking about electrical circuits, each sentence represents a component of the circuit that can be on or off. In this case, when an argument \Gamma \;/\; A is classically valid, it means that whenever all the components represented by sentences in \Gamma are on, then the component represented by A must also be on. A counterexample to the argument would be some way of setting things up so that all the premise components are on and the conclusion component is off.

What about arguments \Gamma \;/\; A where \Gamma is the empty set of premises? Remember, we can have arguments with no premises. What does it take for a valuation to be a counterexample to one of these? The same thing it takes to be a counterexample to any argument: it has to make all the premises take the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and the conclusion take value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. To interpret this, we need to remember logicians' weird habit 2: if there are no Xs, then "all Xs are Ys" automatically counts as true. So when there are no premises, "all the premises take value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} on this valuation" is always true, no matter what valuation we're looking at. That means that, for a valuation to be a counterexample to an argument \;/\; A with no premises, all it needs to do is take the sentence A to the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. The none premises are automatic.

That means that an argument \;/\; A is classically valid whenever the sentence A takes the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} on every Boolean valuation. Such sentences are called theorems. Just like "valid" or "invalid", "theorem" is always relative to a logic. A sentence A might be a theorem in one logic but not another, because the argument \;/\; A is valid in the first logic but not the other. Indeed, we'll see examples like that soon.
A theorem of a logic is a sentence A such that the argument \;/\; A is valid in the logic.

One notable theorem of classical logic is p \lor 
eg p. In setting up our Boolean valuations, we forced every sentence to be true or false on each valuation. Since negation takes falsity to truth, on every valuation one or the other of p and 
eg p is true. And a disjunction is true when at least one of the disjuncts is true. So p \lor 
eg p comes out true no matter what value p has. This appears in the resulting logic: \;/\; p \lor 
eg p is a valid argument.

In fact, we can go farther. For any sentence A, the sentence A \lor 
eg A is a classical theorem. Make sure you see how to interpret this. There's no particular sentence "A \lor 
eg A" in our language; A isn't part of the language at all. Rather, what I'm saying is that p \lor 
eg p is a classical theorem, and q \lor 
eg q is a classical theorem, and (p \land q) \lor 
eg (p \land q) is a classical theorem, and 
eg 
eg (p \lor 
eg r) \lor 
eg 
eg 
eg (p \lor 
eg r) is a classical theorem, and so on. I'm using A as a variable, to make a claim about lots of different sentences all at once.

Before we move on, let's look at one more example. Think about the argument p, 
eg p \;/\; q. Is this classically valid? To find out, we need to think whether it has any classical counterexamples. A classical counterexample would be a Boolean valuation v where v(p) = v(
eg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, since it would need to make both premises true and the conclusion not true. But this can't happen; if v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, then v(
eg p) can't also be \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, because of how negation is handled in Boolean valuations. So there are no classical counterexamples to p, 
eg p \;/\; q. This argument is classically valid.

That sometimes can throw people for a loop; it seems like p and \neg p have nothing to do with q! So how can the argument be valid? This is where it's important to remember how logic works: we lay out precise and clear definitions, and we see where they follow. The way we've set up classical logic, it just falls out that p, \neg p \;/\; q is valid. If it seems to you like arguments like this shouldn't be valid, that might give you a reason to explore logics other than classical logic. This is one of the ways different logics will differ from each other.

Now, let's look at our second example logic. This will work with a similar idea: we're going to think of valuations as something like possible worlds, and we're going to think of an argument as trying to guarantee the truth of its conclusion given the truth of all its premises. But we're now going to use strong Kleene valuations instead of Boolean valuations. Recall that the strong Kleene structure has three values: \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. We can assume that \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} still represents truth, and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} still falsity. But what about \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}? As I presented the strong Kleene structure last time, I mentioned that this value can be interpreted in different ways. For now, let's assume we're interpreting it as something that isn't true: perhaps as neither true nor false, say. So we're supposing that \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} is the only true value in this structure. Then in our new logic, a counterexample to \Gamma \;/\; A is a strong Kleene valuation that takes every premise in \Gamma to the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and takes A to either \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. This logic is called K3. (The "K" here is for "Kleene", and the "3" is for the number of values in the strong Kleene structure.)

As in classical logic, p \land q \;/\; p is valid in K3. By revisiting (or recalling) the strong Kleene table for \land , you can see that for any valuation v, if v(p) is either \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, then v(p \land q) must also be either \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. So there is no valuation that is a counterexample to this argument. Also as in classical logic, p \lor q \;/\; p is invalid in K3. For example, take a valuation w with w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and w(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. Then we have w(p \lor q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}; so this is a counterexample to the argument. (There is another value p could have in counterexamples to this argument as well; do you see it?)

Again as in classical logic, p, 
eg p \;/\; q is valid in K3. To have a K3 counterexample to this argument, we would need a strong Kleene valuation that assigned \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} to both p and 
eg p (as well as assigning some other value to q). But no strong Kleene valuation can assign \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} to both p and 
eg p. So there is no K3 counterexample to this argument.

Despite these similarities, however, classical logic and K3 are not the same. For example, we've seen that \;/\; p \lor 
eg p is classically valid. But it is not K3 valid. That is to say, p \lor 
eg p is not a theorem of K3. Take any strong Kleene valuation v where v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. Then since 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, we also have v(
eg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. And since \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} \lor \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, this gives v(p \lor 
eg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. That is, assuming our interpretation of \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} as neither true nor false, on any valuation where p itself is neither true nor false, p \lor 
eg p is also neither true nor false. And if it's neither true nor false, then in particular it ain't true. So we have a K3 counterexample to \;/\; p \lor 
eg p.

Let's now look at a third example logic, different from both classical logic and K3. Like K3, this third logic makes use of strong Kleene valuations, but it involves treating the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} differently. Suppose that, instead of treating \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} as a value for things that are neither true nor false, we instead treat it as a value for things that are both true and false. Then, if we want our valid arguments to preserve truth, we would say that a counterexample to an argument \Gamma \;/\; A is a strong Kleene valuation that takes every premise in \Gamma to one of the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, and the conclusion A to \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. The logic that results from this is known as LP. (This stands for the "Logic of Paradox"; it was given this name in the late 1970s by the English/Australian logician Graham Priest. The logic itself seems to have been first studied by the Argentinian/USian logician Florencio Asenjo in the early 1950s.)

Important: Even though K3 and LP use the very same valuations, built on the very same structure---the strong Kleene structure---they are different logics. This is because of the different ways counterexamples are determined.

For example, LP is like classical logic, and unlike K3, in counting \;/\; p \lor \neg p as valid. This is because, in a strong Kleene valuation, the sentence p \lor \neg p always takes either the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. Either way, the valuation is not an LP counterexample to this argument. As we've seen, though, if it takes value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} on a valuation, then that valuation is a K3 counterexample to the argument.

On the other hand, LP differs from both classical logic and K3 in holding the argument p, \neg p \;/\; q to be invalid. Take a strong Kleene valuation v where v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}; such a valuation is an LP counterexample to this argument.

The argument p, 
eg p \;/\; q is sometimes known as explosion. It gets such a dramatic name because it seems to suggest that any old contradiction, like having p and 
eg p together, should entail absolutely anything else. And indeed, in classical logic and K3 this is the case: for any sentences A, B at all, A, 
eg A \;/\; B is valid in both classical logic and K3. From a contradiction, anything follows. This is not so in LP, as we've seen. As explosion seems like a very odd kind of argument to many people, this is often held up as a way in which LP more closely matches some of our intuitions about good and bad arguments than some other logics.

This nice feature, though, comes with a bit of a dark side. Think about the following line of reasoning: "The rabbit either went left or it went right. It didn't go left. Therefore, it went right". This line of reasoning strikes many people as if it should be valid: the truth of its premises, it seems, suffices for the truth of its conclusion. And since the argument just turns on "or" and "not", it seems that we can represent it at least decently well in our language, as something like p \lor q, \neg p \;/\; q.

This kind of argument has been studied for thousands of years; it's much much older than the kind of logic we're doing. It's often called "disjunctive syllogism" nowadays; the ancient Stoics knew it as the "fifth indemonstrable". Sextus Empiricus (in the secondish century CE, in his Outlines of Pyrrhonism I.69) had this to say, talking about the earlier Stoic Chrysippus (in the secondish century BCE):

"[Chrysippus] declares that the dog makes use of the fifth complex indemonstrable syllogism when, on arriving at a spot where three ways meet\ldots , after smelling at the two roads by which the quarry did not pass, he rushes off at once by the third without stopping to smell. For, says the old writer, the dog implicitly reasons thus: 'The animal went either by this road, or by that, or by the other: but it did not go by this or that, therefore he went the other way'".

This argument is classically valid, and valid in K3, but not valid in LP. Let's see why. To have a classical counterexample to the argument, we would need a Boolean valuation v where v(p \lor q) = v(\neg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. Let's think about what such a valuation would have to be like. We know v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. What about v(p)? Well, since v(\neg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, we must have v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. But then both p and q take the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and this means that p \lor q must take the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} as well. That is, no Boolean valuation can meet the criteria we need for it to be a counterexample to this argument. As a result, the argument is classically valid.

What about a strong Kleene valuation? Well, we have a strong Kleene valuation w where w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and w(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. This gives us w(\neg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and w(p \lor q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. So this valuation is an LP counterexample to the argument, and the argument is LP invalid. But it is not a K3 counterexample, since it does not make the premises of the argument take the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. Indeed, there is no K3 counterexample to this argument.

The reason p \lor q, 
eg p \;/\; q is invalid in LP is the same reason that p, 
eg p \;/\; q is invalid: because LP allows for p and 
eg p to be true at the same time. This lenience towards contradictions is something we looked at a bit when we introduced Dunn-Belnap valuations, and if we use strong Kleene valuations in the way suggested by LP, we can see it here as well.

fr:title.text: What does this tell us about strength?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: What does this tell us about strength?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: That's the key fact that we need. Getting there depended on a few things: we had to think in detail about all the tables for all the connectives in both structures, as well as about which values count as designated in both matrices. But as a result of looking through that detail, we've now got something we can use.

Take any argument that is invalid in classical logic. Since it is invalid in classical logic, it must have a classical counterexample. But we have just seen that that means that it also has a K3 counterexample: the very same valuation that is its classical counterexample. So that means that it must also be invalid in K3.

So any argument invalid in classical logic is also invalid in K3. That means that any argument valid in K3 must also be valid in classical logic: if it wasn't valid in classical logic, we've seen that it would have to also be invalid in K3. That is, K3 is a subset of classical logic: K3 is weaker than classical logic.

There's no magic tricks here! This is just a matter of moving slowly and methodically through the ideas we have so far, and seeing how they fit together. But the result gives us a discovery: we now know that K3 is weaker than classical logic.

Moreover, you already know from last time that K3 is not stronger than classical logic, since the argument \;/\; p \lor \neg p is valid in classical logic but not in K3. So we've now got the full story about the relationship between these two logics in terms of strength: K3 is weaker than classical logic, but not vice versa.
